Welcome back to Wall Power. It\xe2\x80\x99s the slowest of slow weeks in the art world, but the late summer quiet belies the rush of activity behind the scenes as galleries install their September shows and auction houses await final decisions from the season\xe2\x80\x99s biggest collections. Plenty of property is already booked for the fall, but the two highest-profile estates were still consulting with their advisors as late as last week. Welcome back to Wall Power. It\xe2\x80\x99s the slowest of slow weeks in the art world, but the late summer quiet belies the rush of activity behind the scenes as galleries install their September shows and auction houses await final decisions from the season\xe2\x80\x99s biggest collections. Plenty of property is already booked for the fall, but the two highest-profile estates were still consulting with their advisors as late as last week.

In the meantime, I\xe2\x80\x99m sharing my close read of Michael Findlay \xe2\x80\x99s new memoir about dealing art in \xe2\x80\x9960s and early \xe2\x80\x9970s New York. Michael is best known of late for his role in the sale of Harry and Linda Macklowe \xe2\x80\x99s collection in 2021 and 2022. Since the Macklowes had such an acrimonious divorce\xe2\x80\x94and they could not agree on anything, including how to split their treasured art collection\xe2\x80\x94a judge appointed Findlay as the receiver. His job was to maximize the sale value so that the proceeds could be split between the warring parties. The sales ended up totaling $922 million .

If you get a group of dealers and art advisors going on the subject, someone is going to start speculating about the fees that the judge awarded Findlay. Even a small slice\xe2\x80\x940.5 percent? 1 percent? 1.5 percent?\xe2\x80\x94is still a nice score for an art dealer. But Findlay\xe2\x80\x99s book isn\xe2\x80\x99t about all of that. It isn\xe2\x80\x99t even really about dealing art, though it chronicles his experiences arriving in New York in 1964 as a college dropout through the dawn of Studio 54 in 1977. It\xe2\x80\x99s about the joy of art and the art world\xe2\x80\x94something Findlay suspects has gotten a little lost in all of this talk about money. We\xe2\x80\x99ll get to all of that.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR
Iconic Collection welcomes you to the cutting-edge world of luxury brands and experiences. More than just shopping centers, these are destinations for the next generation of consumers where traditional retail is reimagined through innovation, sustainability, community partnerships, and one-of-a-kind experiences. Each Iconic Collection property provides visitors with high-profile retailers, eclectic culinary feats, and cultural concepts in inspiring settings. Furthermore, Iconic magazine extends this mission and commitment to print. Every issue examines the world of fashion, tech, retail, and beyond. Uncover upcoming trends, meet industry game changers, learn the art and story behind each Iconic Collection property, and so much more.

  • Len Riggio dies at 83: Art collector Len Riggio , the co-founder of Barnes & Noble, passed away today. With a net worth that once climbed into the hundreds of millions of dollars, Riggio was a long-standing buyer of fine art, as well as a trustee of Dia Art Foundation for eight years, during which the institution built a home for its permanent collection in Beacon, New York. \xe2\x80\x9cHe started buying art from Arne Glimcher ,\xe2\x80\x9d a former Dia board member remembered, \xe2\x80\x9cand then Jay Chiat got him involved in Dia and the passion grew.\xe2\x80\x9d Riggio\xe2\x80\x99s home on Park Avenue and a 13-acre Hamptons estate contain a fair bit of his art collection. The family sold their oceanfront Palm Beach home earlier this summer for $96 million.

  • Asia Week approaches : Starting on September 16, it will be Asia Week in New York, as galleries descend upon the city bringing a wide variety of Ancient and Contemporary art from China, Korea, Japan, and South and Southeast Asia. Auction houses\xe2\x80\x94seven of them!\xe2\x80\x94will also hold sales across the various categories. There seems to be a growing interest in woodblock prints.

    Christie\xe2\x80\x99s and Bonhams both have examples of Hokusai \xe2\x80\x99s Great Wave , priced at $500,000 and $700,000 respectively, although these incredibly recognizable prints have gone for as much as $2.7 million. A much more modern version of this long-standing technique will be on view at Scholten Japanese Art, which is showing the Stipanich family collection of 20th century master Kawase Hasui \xe2\x80\x99s woodblock prints.

    There will, of course, be a great deal of ceramics on view from a wide range of periods and regions. One of the more interesting events is a joint group exhibition put on by Fu Qiumeng Fine Art and Hollis Taggart Gallery\xe2\x80\x94a retrospective and expansion of the 1997 exhibition Asian Tradition/Modern Expression. Including art from the 17th through 20th centuries, the show highlights traditional ink paintings and the ways that Chinese artists in the West brought their own understanding of Chinese art history to bear while engaging with Abstract Expressionism.
Findlay\xe2\x80\x99s Wake
A new memoir by legendary art dealer Michael Findlay looks back at the wild art world of 1960s New York, when buildings in Soho sold for a song and the appreciation of a work of art outweighed its investment potential.
MARION MANEKER
After a lifetime as a middle man for great works of art, Michael Findlay wants us to actually see and appreciate them again\xe2\x80\x94and maybe have a little more fun while doing it. Findlay, of course, is still selling art privately at Acquavella Gallery, a role he\xe2\x80\x99s held for almost a quarter-century, long after most of his contemporaries have retired or passed on. At 79 years old, he can boast of having presided over Christie\xe2\x80\x99s Impressionist and Modern department in New York during the boom of the 1980s\xe2\x80\x94when works by Van Gogh , such as the Portrait of Dr. Gachet , were selling for spectacular prices\xe2\x80\x94as well as surviving the bust of the 1990s.

But Findlay doesn\xe2\x80\x99t want to brag about his art dealing triumphs. Rather, in his new memoir, Portrait of the Art Dealer as a Young Man , he is continuing an unconscious project to invite others to experience the joys and satisfactions of art. A previous book, The Value of Art , remains the single best explanation of how the art market really works, told in plain, unpretentious language. Its spiritual sequel, Seeing Slowly , is more of an admonition that one should take one\xe2\x80\x99s time to experience art without preconceptions or intimidation. \xe2\x80\x9cThe first two books were written specifically to try to balance and help people,\xe2\x80\x9d Findlay told me from his weekend house near Goshen, New York. \xe2\x80\x9cIn approaching the collecting of art, the appreciation of art and the engagement with art, and trying to take some of the mystery out of it that keeps people out of galleries.\xe2\x80\x9d
A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR
Iconic Collection welcomes you to the cutting-edge world of luxury brands and experiences. More than just shopping centers, these are destinations for the next generation of consumers where traditional retail is reimagined through innovation, sustainability, community partnerships, and one-of-a-kind experiences. Each Iconic Collection property provides visitors with high-profile retailers, eclectic culinary feats, and cultural concepts in inspiring settings. Furthermore, Iconic magazine extends this mission and commitment to print. Every issue examines the world of fashion, tech, retail, and beyond. Uncover upcoming trends, meet industry game changers, learn the art and story behind each Iconic Collection property, and so much more.
With Portrait of the Art Dealer as a Young Man , Findlay wants to make another point about participating in the art world through his own life story. Despite his shiny brass-plate r\xc3\xa9sum\xc3\xa9 and London accent, Findlay isn\xe2\x80\x99t quite the posh, privileged character many would assume of a Christie\xe2\x80\x99s and Acquavella veteran who comingles with billionaires. Yes, he went to a British public school, but only as a middle-class scholarship kid. (His father was a sports journalist and heavy gambler.) He applied to college in North America, and was accepted to Harvard but couldn\xe2\x80\x99t afford the fees. Instead, he lucked into a scholarship at York University in Toronto. After a year, the bohemian Findlay was done: In 1964, he found his way to New York City at the age of 18 with $200 in his pocket and the notion that he might hang around for a few weeks, maybe hit up some poetry readings and jazz clubs.

Instead, Findlay found himself interacting with artists and art collectors in Manhattan, where he landed a job at Richard Feigen \xe2\x80\x99s gallery in SoHo. Feigen, only a decade his senior, would become a towering figure in late 20th century art dealing, providing Findlay with an extraordinary vantage point to start his own career. The rare art dealer with a degree from Harvard Business School, Feigen sold art because he loved it\xe2\x80\x94and for the thrill of the dealmaking and social connections. \xe2\x80\x9cI never heard Richard Feigen sell a work of art with a promise of investment,\xe2\x80\x9d he writes. \xe2\x80\x9cThe postwar generation of collectors active in New York in the 1960s and 1970s were paying what they could afford, to buy what they wanted to own and keep. This applied equally to those with traditional tastes who collected Pissarro and Ce\xcc\x81zanne , as it did to those who collected Pop art.\xe2\x80\x9d

At the time, the art world in New York was a relatively small place\xe2\x80\x94there were galleries uptown where art was shown and sold, but never as an investment. Over the course of the decade, the scene began to grow and eventually, to change. Attracted to the cheap real estate and the presence of artists living in lofts, Feigen bought two buildings in SoHo, where he installed Findlay to run his downtown outpost. Much of Findlay\xe2\x80\x99s memoir, written in a picaresque style that will be familiar to readers of a certain type of novel in the late \xe2\x80\x9960s, takes us through the neighborhood\xe2\x80\x99s beginnings, when having a show was still more important than selling the work.

Findlay\xe2\x80\x99s long hair, obvious social skills, and good-natured detachment served him well in New York. The memoir recounts his many adventures, including multiple stints as a model, attempts to open a nightclub, a brief episode dealing weed, the many models he dated (and married!)\xe2\x80\x94not to mention the year he dated Carly Simon \xe2\x80\x94and, of course, the artists and collectors he knew. A regular at Max\xe2\x80\x99s Kansas City, Findlay was part of Andy Warhol \xe2\x80\x99s extended circle.

Findlay isn\xe2\x80\x99t preoccupied with name-dropping\xe2\x80\x94but he can toss off a few that really thud. Throughout his memoir, as if to underscore his larger point about the appreciation of art for art\xe2\x80\x99s sake, Findlay discusses numerous artists whose names will mean nothing to you or me. He treats them all with equal weight, because the book is about his encounter with these artists and their work, not about who became a household name.
Artist Interruptus
The story of Portrait of the Art Dealer as a Young Man concludes, somewhat abruptly, in 1977, with Grace Jones taking the stage at Studio 54\xe2\x80\x94the moment, he says, when the \xe2\x80\x9960s truly ended. When we spoke, Findlay told me it was a deliberate choice to not include any details about what happened to any of the book\xe2\x80\x99s characters after that point. But that omission deprives us of a good example of what he wants us to understand.

To wit: One of the artists included in the first show Findlay mounted in SoHo, in October 1968\xe2\x80\x94most likely the first gallery show ever in Soho\xe2\x80\x94was John Baldessari , who had come to him with black-and-white photographs of his conceptual word paintings. Findlay sold some of the works by the other artists in that first show, but no one bit on Baldessari. One of his paintings sat in Findlay\xe2\x80\x99s office for months. \xe2\x80\x9cI loved having that painting behind my desk for $1,200,\xe2\x80\x9d he told me. (Or was it $800? He can\xe2\x80\x99t remember.) But not selling it was not considered a failure. What Findlay doesn\xe2\x80\x99t say in the book is that 40 years later, the painting sold at auction for $4.4 million. I was in the auction room with Findlay when that happened. He exited in high spirits, laughing. No gnashing resentment, just genuine amusement.

Of course, the reader doesn\xe2\x80\x99t need this context to get Findlay\xe2\x80\x99s larger point: the value of all art is social. \xe2\x80\x9cI wanted to provide some navigation,\xe2\x80\x9d he told me. \xe2\x80\x9cI thought if I could show people the zig-zaggy path that I took,\xe2\x80\x9d they would understand that there are other ways to engage with art than through money. (Not that there\xe2\x80\x99s anything wrong with the money; Findlay is an art dealer, after all.) The point here isn\xe2\x80\x99t that Findlay spotted the value long before others. It\xe2\x80\x99s that one should trust one\xe2\x80\x99s own responses to art, which are far more valuable than any market returns one might achieve\xe2\x80\x94or miss out on.

Findlay, in this book at least, is more interested in transporting us back to a particular moment in time, before the art world turned into a market. As an example, Findlay pointed to the collectors of the \xe2\x80\x9960s and \xe2\x80\x9970s who were just having fun buying art. \xe2\x80\x9cThey weren\xe2\x80\x99t spending huge sums of money for them ,\xe2\x80\x9d he told me. \xe2\x80\x9cThey would go to galleries regularly and chat with dealers. They liked what they were buying even though there were plenty of people who thought it was risible.\xe2\x80\x9d And he wants people to understand that you can have fun with it, too. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s not rocket science,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, \xe2\x80\x9cbut you have to look at a lot .\xe2\x80\x9d He implores gallery goers to have confidence that their reactions to art\xe2\x80\x94good or bad\xe2\x80\x94are valid. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd you have to do it yourself.\xe2\x80\x9d
On Sunday, I\xe2\x80\x99m hoping to have a look at another storied art dealer, whose collection will be auctioned in Switzerland this September. The works don\xe2\x80\x99t look cheap by any means, but after the recent bout of inflation, they might be priced for today\xe2\x80\x99s market. At least, there should be a lot of people watching to get a better sense of the Modern market when works by Kirchner , Klee , Giacometti , and other names all go up for sale.

Until then,
Marion
